vocabulary.definition_ar: الصخر الصلب الذي يقع تحت الرواسب المفككة من التربة والمواد الأخرى
vocabulary.example_sentence: Buildings constructed on bedrock are more stable during earthquakes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: قطع مكسورة من الصخور أو مواد البناء أو أشياء أخرى متناثرة
vocabulary.example_sentence: The landslide left debris covering the entire road.

vocabulary.definition_ar: متعلق بالقوى التي تضغط أو تكبس المواد معاً
vocabulary.example_sentence: P-waves are compressional waves that travel fastest through the Earth.

vocabulary.definition_ar: متعلق بمركز الزلزال أو واقع في مركز الزلزال
vocabulary.example_sentence: The epicentral region suffered the most severe damage from the earthquake.

vocabulary.definition_ar: النظرية التي تفسر كيف تنطلق الطاقة المختزنة في الصخور المشوهة فجأة أثناء الزلازل
vocabulary.example_sentence: Elastic rebound theory explains the mechanism behind most earthquake occurrences.

vocabulary.definition_ar: منحدر أو منحدر حاد تشكل بسبب الحركة على طول خط الصدع
vocabulary.example_sentence: The recent earthquake created a visible fault scarp across the valley.

vocabulary.definition_ar: زلزال أصغر يحدث قبل الزلزال الرئيسي الأكبر في نفس المنطقة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Scientists detected several foreshocks before the major earthquake hit the city.

vocabulary.definition_ar: الحركة البطيئة والتدريجية على طول الصدع التي تحدث دون إحداث زلازل.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Fault creep can damage pipelines and roads over many decades.

vocabulary.definition_ar: يسبب أو ينتج عنه حدوث شيء
vocabulary.example_sentence: Tectonic plate movements give rise to most major earthquakes worldwide.

vocabulary.definition_ar: العمق تحت سطح الأرض حيث ينشأ الزلزال
vocabulary.example_sentence: Shallow earthquakes with focal depth less than 70 km cause more damage.

vocabulary.definition_ar: متعلق بقياس ورسم خرائط سطح الأرض ومجال الجاذبية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Geodetic surveys help scientists track slow ground movements before earthquakes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: النقطة الدقيقة تحت الأرض حيث يبدأ الزلزال وتنطلق الطاقة أولاً
vocabulary.example_sentence: The hypocenter was located 15 kilometers below the city center.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حركة سطح الأرض أثناء الزلزال
vocabulary.example_sentence: Engineers study ground motion to design earthquake-resistant buildings.

vocabulary.definition_ar: متعلق بالخطوط على الخريطة التي تربط نقاط شدة الزلزال المتساوية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Isoseismal maps show how earthquake shaking varies across different areas.

vocabulary.definition_ar: زلزال صغير جداً أو اهتزاز أرضي لا يمكن اكتشافه إلا بأجهزة حساسة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Scientists monitor microseisms to study ongoing geological processes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: نظام لقياس التأثيرات والأضرار التي يسببها الزلزال
vocabulary.example_sentence: The intensity scale helps scientists compare earthquake damage across different regions.

vocabulary.definition_ar: خط كنتور مرسوم على خريطة يصل بين الأماكن التي شهدت نفس شدة الزلزال.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Isoseismal lines help engineers identify which areas need stronger buildings.

vocabulary.definition_ar: مقياس قياس للزلازل مبني على إجمالي الطاقة المُطلَقة من انكسار الصدع.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Moment magnitude replaced older scales for describing very large earthquakes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: دراسة الزلازل ما قبل التاريخ باستخدام الأدلة الجيولوجية المحفوظة في الصخور والتربة.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Palaeoseismology helps scientists estimate how often major earthquakes recur.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حدوث زلازل صغيرة جدًا أضعف من أن يشعر بها الناس.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Monitoring microseismicity can reveal hidden faults beneath a city.

vocabulary.definition_ar: ينتشر أو ينتقل عبر وسط، خاصة بالإشارة إلى الموجات الزلزالية
vocabulary.example_sentence: Seismic waves propagate through the Earth's crust at different speeds.

vocabulary.definition_ar: نوع من الصدع حيث يتحرك الجدار المعلق لأسفل نسبة إلى الجدار السفلي
vocabulary.example_sentence: Normal faults typically form in areas where the crust is being stretched.

vocabulary.definition_ar: السرعة التي يتحرك بها الكسر على طول الصدع خلال الزلزال.
vocabulary.example_sentence: A higher rupture velocity often produces stronger shaking over a wider area.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حدث أو ظاهرة تحدث قبل وقد تشير إلى زلزال قادم
vocabulary.example_sentence: Animal behavior changes are sometimes considered earthquake precursors.

vocabulary.definition_ar: نوع من الصدع حيث يتحرك الجدار المعلق لأعلى نسبة إلى الجدار السفلي
vocabulary.example_sentence: Reverse faults occur when rocks are compressed and pushed together.

vocabulary.definition_ar: قسم من خط صدع نشط لم يشهد زلازل حديثة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Scientists monitor seismic gaps as they may indicate future earthquake locations.

vocabulary.definition_ar: مثبت بإحكام وغير مرجح أن يتغير أو يتحرك فجأة
vocabulary.example_sentence: Geologically stable areas have very few earthquakes throughout history.

vocabulary.definition_ar: التباين في سرعة الموجات الزلزالية بحسب الاتجاه الذي تسير فيه.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Seismic anisotropy reveals the alignment of crystals deep inside the earth.

vocabulary.definition_ar: طبقة من الصخر أو الرواسب ذات خصائص متسقة داخلياً
vocabulary.example_sentence: Geologists studied each stratum to understand the area's geological history.

vocabulary.definition_ar: حدوث وتوزيع الزلازل في منطقة معينة عبر الزمن
vocabulary.example_sentence: High seismicity in the region requires strict building codes and preparation.

vocabulary.definition_ar: التراكم التدريجي للضغط في الصخور قبل أن تنكسر وتسبب الزلازل
vocabulary.example_sentence: Strain accumulation along fault lines increases earthquake risk over time.

vocabulary.definition_ar: معرض للتأثر بشيء أو تجربة شيء ضار
vocabulary.example_sentence: Buildings on soft soil are more susceptible to earthquake damage.

vocabulary.definition_ar: التسبب في بدء الزلزال، غالبًا بسبب قوى تكتونية أو بركانية.
vocabulary.example_sentence: A volcanic eruption can trigger a quake in nearby fault systems.

vocabulary.definition_ar: نوع من الصدع حيث تتحرك الصخور أفقياً متجاوزة بعضها البعض
vocabulary.example_sentence: The San Andreas Fault is a famous strike-slip fault in California.

vocabulary.definition_ar: طريقة لتحديد موقع الزلزال باستخدام بيانات من ثلاث محطات رصد زلزالي أو أكثر
vocabulary.example_sentence: Seismologists use triangulation to pinpoint the exact epicenter of earthquakes.

vocabulary.definition_ar: انتشار طاقة الموجة الزلزالية كلما ابتعدت أكثر عن مركز الزلزال.
vocabulary.example_sentence: Wave dispersion reduces the intensity of shaking at distant locations.

paragraphs: Deep beneath the bustling streets of Riyadh and the ancient corridors of Makkah, powerful forces are constantly at work. The Earth's crust, which appears solid and unchanging to us, is actually in continuous motion, moving on massive rock slabs called tectonic plates. When these plates suddenly shift or collide, they *trigger* earthquakes that can *devastate* entire communities within seconds.

Scientists have been working to *unravel* the mysteries of seismic activity for centuries, and their research has revealed fascinating patterns. The majority of earthquakes occur along what geologists call the 'Ring of Fire' around the Pacific Ocean, where tectonic plates *converge* and create intense pressure. However, the Arabian Peninsula sits on its own tectonic plate, which explains why the region experiences occasional seismic events. In 2009, residents of Harrat Lunayyir, northwest of Medina, experienced thousands of small earthquakes over several months, reminding everyone that even seemingly stable ground can *fluctuate* unexpectedly.

Modern earthquake detection has become incredibly sophisticated, allowing scientists to *monitor* seismic waves in real-time using networks of sensitive instruments called seismometers. These devices can *detect* ground movements so small that they're measured in millimeters, yet they provide crucial data that helps researchers *assess* earthquake risks. When an earthquake strikes, it sends out different types of waves through the Earth's layers. Primary waves, or P-waves, travel fastest and arrive first, while secondary waves, or S-waves, follow behind and typically cause more damage. This *phenomenon* allows seismologists to quickly determine an earthquake's location and magnitude.

The *magnitude* of an earthquake, measured on the Richter scale, indicates the amount of energy released during the event. Each number on the scale represents a tenfold increase in amplitude, meaning a magnitude 6.0 earthquake produces waves ten times larger than a 5.0 earthquake. While small earthquakes barely register to human senses, major quakes above magnitude 7.0 can *rupture* the ground surface and topple buildings. The most devastating earthquake in recorded history occurred in 1556 in China, killing approximately 830,000 people, though modern building codes and emergency preparedness have significantly reduced such casualties.

Despite remarkable advances in seismology, predicting exactly when and where earthquakes will occur remains one of science's greatest challenges. Researchers continue to *investigate* various warning signs, from unusual animal behavior to subtle changes in groundwater levels, hoping to develop reliable forecasting methods. Until then, the best defense against earthquake damage lies in proper building construction, community preparedness, and education about how to respond when the ground beneath our feet begins to shake.

In the heart of Tokyo, a 634-meter tower *sways* gracefully during earthquakes, moving up to two meters at its peak while remaining completely safe. The Tokyo Skytree, like many modern structures, has been designed to *accommodate* seismic forces rather than resist them—a revolutionary approach that experts believe could *transform* construction practices worldwide.

Traditional earthquake engineering focused on creating rigid structures that would *withstand* ground shaking through sheer strength. However, engineers have discovered that buildings which can *flex* and move with seismic waves often *fare* better than their inflexible counterparts. This paradigm shift has led to innovative technologies such as base isolation systems, where buildings are literally separated from their foundations by layers of rubber and steel. When earthquakes *strike*, the ground moves beneath the structure while the building itself remains relatively stable. Japanese researchers report that buildings equipped with these systems experience 80% less shaking during major seismic events.

One of the most fascinating developments involves *viscous* dampers—devices filled with thick fluid that absorb earthquake energy. These dampers work similarly to car shock absorbers, converting the kinetic energy of building movement into heat. Engineers *incorporate* hundreds of these devices into skyscrapers, creating what seismologists call 'smart buildings' that can *adapt* to different types of ground motion. The effectiveness of this technology became evident during the 2011 Tōhoku earthquake in Japan, when buildings equipped with advanced damping systems remained operational while conventional structures suffered significant damage.

The field of earthquake engineering continues to *evolve* as scientists develop new materials and construction techniques. Shape-memory alloys—metals that return to their original form after deformation—are being integrated into building frames. These materials allow structures to 'remember' their original shape and automatically repair minor earthquake damage. Additionally, researchers are experimenting with *artificial* intelligence systems that can predict building behavior during earthquakes and automatically adjust damping systems in real-time. Such innovations *signify* a future where buildings don't just survive earthquakes but actively respond to them.

As urban populations grow and cities expand into seismically active regions, the importance of earthquake-resistant design becomes increasingly critical. The lessons learned from countries like Japan and Chile, where stringent building codes have dramatically reduced earthquake casualties, demonstrate that proper engineering can save thousands of lives. While we cannot prevent earthquakes, we can certainly build structures that *render* them far less destructive to human communities.

sections.content_ar: الشرط الثاني يُستخدم للحديث عن المواقف الخيالية أو غير الحقيقية في الحاضر أو المستقبل. هذه مواقف من غير المرجح أن تحدث أو مستحيلة الحدوث
sections.content_en:
The Second Conditional is used to talk about imaginary or unreal situations in the present or future. These are situations that are unlikely to happen or impossible.

We use it to:
• Talk about hypothetical situations
• Give advice
• Express wishes about present situations

The Second Conditional has two parts:
1. If clause (condition) - uses past simple
2. Main clause (result) - uses would + base verb

Note: We use were for all persons with the verb 'be' in formal English.

sections.content: If + subject + past simple, subject + would + base verb

items.sentence: If scientists could predict earthquakes perfectly, they would save thousands of lives.
items.translation_ar: لو استطاع العلماء التنبؤ بالزلازل بشكل مثالي، لأنقذوا آلاف الأرواح

items.sentence: If I were a seismologist, I would study earthquake patterns all day.
items.translation_ar: لو كنت عالم زلازل، لدرست أنماط الزلازل طوال اليوم

items.sentence: People would feel safer if buildings were more earthquake-resistant.
items.translation_ar: سيشعر الناس بأمان أكبر لو كانت المباني أكثر مقاومة للزلازل

items.explanation_ar: نستخدم 'were' مع جميع الضمائر في الشرط الثاني
لا نستخدم 'will' في جملة الشرط، بل الماضي البسيط

items.wrong: If we could predict earthquakes, we will save lives.
items.correct: If we could predict earthquakes, we would save lives.
